q:
The bob of a simple pendulum has mass 2 g and a charge of 5.0 μC. It is at rest in a uniform horizontal electric field of intensity 2000 V/m At equilibrium, the angle that the pendulum makes with the vertical is:
take g=10 m/s2
question:
The bob of a simple pendulum has mass 2 g and a charge of 5.0 μC. It is at rest in a uniform horizontal electric field of intensity 2000 V/m At equilibrium, the angle that the pendulum makes with the vertical is:
take g=10 m/s2
solution:
tan⁡θ=QEmg=5×10-6×20002×10-3×10=12
⇒θ=tan-1⁡0.5

subject: Physics
q: Two charges of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>Q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>Q</mi></math> are situated at the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> respectively. The electric flux through a sphere of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>a</mi></math> having centre at origin is:

The electric flux through a sphere of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>a</mi></math> having centre at origin is:
solution:
As can be seen from the figure above, only the 5Q charge is inside the spherical region.

The formula to calculate the flux through the surface is given by

ϕ=qencε0   ...1

From equation (1), the flux through the given surface can be calculated as follows:

ϕ=5Qε0

subject: Physics
q:
Two small equal point charges of magnitude $q$ are suspended from a common point on the ceiling by insulating mass less strings of equal lengths. They come to equilibrium with each string making angle $\theta$ from the vertical. If the mass of each charge is $m$, then the electrostatic potential at the centre of line joining them will be
$$
\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0}=k\right) \text {. }
$$
question:
Two small equal point charges of magnitude $q$ are suspended from a common point on the ceiling by insulating mass less strings of equal lengths. They come to equilibrium with each string making angle $\theta$ from the vertical. If the mass of each charge is $m$, then the electrostatic potential at the centre of line joining them will be
$$
\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0}=k\right) \text {. }
$$
solution: In equilibrium, $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathrm{T} \sin \theta$ $$ \begin{aligned} & m g=T \cos \theta \\ & \tan \theta=\frac{F_e}{m g}=\frac{q^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 x^2 \times m g} \\ & \therefore x=\sqrt{\frac{q^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 \tan \theta m g}} \end{aligned} $$ Electric potential at the centre of the line $$ \mathrm{V}=\frac{\mathrm{kq}}{\mathrm{x} / 2}+\frac{\mathrm{kq}}{\mathrm{x} / 2}=4 \sqrt{\mathrm{kmg} / \tan \theta} $$

subject: Physics
q:
A thin metallic wire having cross sectional area of 10-4 m2 is used to make a ring of radius 30 cm. A positive charge of 2π C is uniformly distributed over the ring, while another positive charge of 30 pC is kept at the centre of the ring. The tension in the ring is _______ N; provided that the ring does not get deformed (neglect the influence of gravity).

(Given, 14πϵ0=9×109 SI units)
question:
A thin metallic wire having cross sectional area of 10-4 m2 is used to make a ring of radius 30 cm. A positive charge of 2π C is uniformly distributed over the ring, while another positive charge of 30 pC is kept at the centre of the ring. The tension in the ring is _______ N; provided that the ring does not get deformed (neglect the influence of gravity).

(Given, 14πϵ0=9×109 SI units)
solution:
The linear charge density of the ring is given by

λ=Q2πR   ...1

With reference to the above diagram, for the equilibrium condition of the ring, it can be written that

2 Tsindθ2=14πε0q0R2·λRdθ

⇒T=14πε0q0QR2×2π [As for very small angles sindθ2=dθ2]

=9×1092π×30×10-12(0.30)2×2π

=9×10-3×309×10-2=3 N

subject: Physics
q: An electric dipole has fixed dipole moment <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math>, which makes angle <math><mi>θ</mi></math> with respect to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math>axis. When subjected to an electric field <math><msub><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>E</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo></math> it experiences a torque <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover accent="true"><mi>T</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>τ</mi><mover accent="true"><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>. When subjected to another electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover accent="true"><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mi> </mi><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mover accent="true"><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> it experiences a torque <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover accent="true"><mi>T</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><msub><mover accent="true"><mi>T</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub></math> . The angle <math><mi>θ</mi></math> is:
question: An electric dipole has fixed dipole moment <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math>, which makes angle <math><mi>θ</mi></math> with respect to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math>axis. When subjected to an electric field <math><msub><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>E</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo></math> it experiences a torque <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover accent="true"><mi>T</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>τ</mi><mover accent="true"><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>. When subjected to another electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover accent="true"><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mi> </mi><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mover accent="true"><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> it experiences a torque <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover accent="true"><mi>T</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><msub><mover accent="true"><mi>T</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub></math> . The angle <math><mi>θ</mi></math> is:
solution:
P→=Pcos⁡θi^+Psin⁡θj^

T→=Pcos⁡θi^+Psin⁡θj^×Ei^

=-PEsin⁡θk^

T→2=Pcos⁡θi^+Psin⁡θj^×3Ej^

= 3PEcos⁡θk^

T→2= -T→1

∴    sin⁡θ= 3cos⁡θ

∴    tan⁡θ= 3

∴   θ=60o

q: Consider two charged metallic spheres <math><msub><mrow><mi>S</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>S</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> of radii <math><msub><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> respectively. question: Consider two charged metallic spheres <math><msub><mrow><mi>S</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>S</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> of radii <math><msub><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> respectively. The electric fields <math><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> (on <math><msub><mrow><mi>S</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> ) and <math><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> (on <math><msub><mrow><mi>S</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> ) on their surfaces are such that <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> Then the ratio <math><msub><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> (on <math><msub><mrow><mi>S</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> )<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>/</mo></math><math><msub><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> (on <math><msub><mrow><mi>S</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> ) of the electrostatic potentials on each sphere is:
solution:
Given, E1E2=R1R2
Then ratio of potential would be,V1V2=E1R1E2R2=R1R2×R1R2=R1R22

subject: Physics
q: Two identical charged spheres are suspended by strings of equal lengths. The string make an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>37</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with each other. When suspended in a liquid of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the angle remains same. If density of material of the sphere is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the dielectric constant of the liquid is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>tan</mi><mn>37</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: Two identical charged spheres are suspended by strings of equal lengths. The string make an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>37</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with each other. When suspended in a liquid of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the angle remains same. If density of material of the sphere is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the dielectric constant of the liquid is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>tan</mi><mn>37</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
For first case:

Tcosθ=mg and 

Tsinθ=Fe

Therefore, tanθ=Femg

tanθ=FeρBVg....(i)

For second case:

tanθ=FekρBVg-ρLVg.....(ii) 

From Eq. (i) & (ii), we get

ρBVg=ρB-ρLkVg

⇒1.4 = 0.7 k 

⇒k = 2

subject: Physics
q: A simple pendulum has a bob with mass $m$ and charge $q$. The pendulum string has negligible mass. When a uniform and horizontal electric field $\vec{E}$ is applied, the tension in the string changes. The final tension in the string, when pendulum attains an equilibrium position is $\_\_\_\_$. (g: acceleration due to gravity)
question: A simple pendulum has a bob with mass $m$ and charge $q$. The pendulum string has negligible mass. When a uniform and horizontal electric field $\vec{E}$ is applied, the tension in the string changes. The final tension in the string, when pendulum attains an equilibrium position is $\_\_\_\_$. (g: acceleration due to gravity)
solution:
The charged bob experiences two forces at equilibrium:
Gravitational force: $F_g = mg$ (downward)
Electric force: $F_E = qE$ (horizontal, in direction of field)
These forces are perpendicular to each other. The string tension must balance their resultant.
At equilibrium, the string makes angle $\theta$ with vertical such that:
Vertical: $T\cos\theta = mg$
Horizontal: $T\sin\theta = qE$
The net force from gravity and electric field is $\sqrt{(mg)^2 + (qE)^2}$
Therefore, the tension in the string is: $T = \sqrt{m^2g^2 + q^2E^2}$

subject: Physics
q:
Two metal plates $(A, B)$ are kept horizontally with separation of $\left(\dfrac{12}{\pi}\right)$ cm, with plate A on the top. An atomizer jet sprays oil (density $1.5$ g/cm$^3$) droplets of radius $1$ mm horizontally. All oil droplets carry a charge $5$ nC. The potentials $V_A$ and $V_B$ are required on plates A and B respectively in order to ensure the droplets do not descend. The values of $V_A$ and $V_B$ are _______.
(Neglect the air resistance to the droplets and take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
question:
Two metal plates $(A, B)$ are kept horizontally with separation of $\left(\dfrac{12}{\pi}\right)$ cm, with plate A on the top. An atomizer jet sprays oil (density $1.5$ g/cm$^3$) droplets of radius $1$ mm horizontally. All oil droplets carry a charge $5$ nC. The potentials $V_A$ and $V_B$ are required on plates A and B respectively in order to ensure the droplets do not descend. The values of $V_A$ and $V_B$ are _______.
(Neglect the air resistance to the droplets and take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
For the oil droplets to not descend, the upward electric force must balance the downward gravitational force.

$qE = mg$

The mass of the oil droplet is given by:

$m = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho$

Substituting the given values $r = 10^{-3}$ m and $\rho = 1500$ kg/m$^3$:

$m = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi (10^{-3})^3 \times 1500 = 2 \pi \times 10^{-6}$ kg

The electric field $E$ required is:

$E = \dfrac{mg}{q} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 10^{-6} \times 10}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = 4000 \pi$ V/m

The potential difference $\Delta V$ between the plates is:

$\Delta V = E \times d = 4000 \pi \times \left(\dfrac{12}{\pi} \times 10^{-2}\right) = 480$ V

Since the charge on the droplet is positive, the electric field must be directed upwards to provide an upward force. Electric field lines point from higher potential to lower potential, so the bottom plate B must be at a higher potential than the top plate A.

$V_B - V_A = 480$ V

Checking the given options, $V_A = 100$ V and $V_B = 580$ V satisfies this condition.

Answer: $100 \, V$ and $580 \, V$

subject: Physics
q: Ten charges are placed on the circumference of a circle of radius R with constant angular separation between successive charges. Alternate charges<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>9</mn></math> have charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>+</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> each, while <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>10</mn></math> have charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>–</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> each. The potential V and the electric field E at the centre of the circle are respectively : (Take<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> at infinity)
question: Ten charges are placed on the circumference of a circle of radius R with constant angular separation between successive charges. Alternate charges<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>9</mn></math> have charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>+</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> each, while <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>10</mn></math> have charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>–</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> each. The potential V and the electric field E at the centre of the circle are respectively : (Take<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> at infinity)
solution:
vnet=5kqR+5k(-q)R
vnet=0, Qnet=0
Enet=0, by symmetry

subject: Physics
q: An electron is released from rest near an infinite non-conducting sheet of uniform charge density ' $-\sigma$ '. The rate of change of de-Broglie wave length associated with the electron varies inversely as $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ power of time. The numerical value of $n$ is ________.
question: An electron is released from rest near an infinite non-conducting sheet of uniform charge density ' $-\sigma$ '. The rate of change of de-Broglie wave length associated with the electron varies inversely as $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ power of time. The numerical value of $n$ is ________.
solution:
Let the momentum of $\mathrm{e}^{-}$at any time t is p and its de-broglie wavelength is $\lambda$.
Then, $\mathrm{p}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\lambda}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{dp}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{-\mathrm{h}}{\lambda^2} \frac{\mathrm{~d} \lambda}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & \mathrm{ma}=\mathrm{F}=-\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\lambda} \frac{\mathrm{~d} \lambda}{\mathrm{dt}} \quad[\mathrm{~m}=\text { mass of } \mathrm{e}]
\end{aligned}$
Where, -ve sign represents decrease in $\lambda$ with time
$\mathrm{ma}=\frac{-\mathrm{h}}{(\mathrm{~h} / \mathrm{p})^2} \frac{\mathrm{~d} \lambda}{\mathrm{dt}}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{a}=-\frac{\mathrm{p}^2}{\mathrm{mh}} \frac{\mathrm{~d} \lambda}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & \mathrm{a}=-\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{~h}} \frac{\mathrm{~d} \lambda}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~d} \lambda}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{\mathrm{ah}}{\mathrm{mv}^2}...(1)
\end{aligned}$
here, $\mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{qE}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{m}} \frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}$
$\mathrm{a}=\frac{\sigma \mathrm{e}}{2 \mathrm{~m} \varepsilon_0}$
and $\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{u}+\mathrm{at}$
$\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{at}$
Substituting values of a \& v in equation (1)
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{d} \lambda}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{2 \mathrm{~h} \varepsilon_0}{\sigma \mathrm{et}^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} \lambda}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=2
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A dipole with two electric charges of $2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ magnitude each, with separation distance $0.5 \mu \mathrm{~m}$, is placed between the plates of a capacitor such that its axis is parallel to an electric field established between the plates when a potential difference of 5 V is applied. Separation between the plates is 0.5 mm. If the dipole is rotated by $30^{\circ}$ from the axis, it tends to realign in the direction due to a torque. The value of torque is :
question: A dipole with two electric charges of $2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ magnitude each, with separation distance $0.5 \mu \mathrm{~m}$, is placed between the plates of a capacitor such that its axis is parallel to an electric field established between the plates when a potential difference of 5 V is applied. Separation between the plates is 0.5 mm. If the dipole is rotated by $30^{\circ}$ from the axis, it tends to realign in the direction due to a torque. The value of torque is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{~d}}=\frac{5}{5 \times 10^{-4}}=10^4 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m} \\ & \tau=\mathrm{PE} \sin \theta
\end{aligned}$
Where $\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{qa}=2 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{-7}$
$=1 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{m}$
$\tau=1 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^4 \times \frac{1}{2}=5 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{m}$

subject: Physics
q: Three point charges q,-2q  and 2q are placed on x axis at a distance x=0,x=34R and x=R respectively from origin as shown. If q=2×10-6 C and R=2 cm, the magnitude of net force experienced by the charge -2q is _____ N.
question: Three point charges q,-2q  and 2q are placed on x axis at a distance x=0,x=34R and x=R respectively from origin as shown. If q=2×10-6 C and R=2 cm, the magnitude of net force experienced by the charge -2q is _____ N.
solution:
The net force on the given charge will be the resultant of the forces due to the other two charges.

Hence, the net force Fn can be calculated as follows:

Fn= 14πε04q2R42-14πε02q23R42=9×109×16q2R24-29=9×109×16×2×10-6 C20.02 m2×349=5440 N

q: Three concentric spherical metallic shells <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi></math> of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> respectively <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo><</mo><mi>b</mi><mo><</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> have surface charge densities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>–</mo><mo> </mo><mi>σ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi></math>, respectively. The shells <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi></math> are at same potential. If the radii of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Y</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, respectively. The radius of shell <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi></math> is _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
question: Three concentric spherical metallic shells <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi></math> of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> respectively <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo><</mo><mi>b</mi><mo><</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> have surface charge densities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>–</mo><mo> </mo><mi>σ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi></math>, respectively. The shells <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi></math> are at same potential. If the radii of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Y</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, respectively. The radius of shell <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi></math> is _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
The charges on the spheres are

 qX=σ4πa2qY=-σ4πb2qZ=σ4πc2 

It is given that VX=VZ

So, 

qX4πε0a+qY4πε0b+qZ4πε0c=qX4πε0c+qY4πε0c+qZ4πε0c⇒σ4πa2a-σ4πb2b+σ4πc2c=σ4π(a2-b2+c2)c⇒c(a-b+c)=(a2-b2+c2)⇒c=a+b=2 cm+3 cm=5 cm

subject: Physics
q: As shown in the figure, a point charge Q is placed at the centre of conducting spherical shell of inner radius a and outer radius b. The electric field due to charge Q in three different regions I, II and III is given by : (I : r < a, II : a < r < b, III : r > b)
question: As shown in the figure, a point charge Q is placed at the centre of conducting spherical shell of inner radius a and outer radius b. The electric field due to charge Q in three different regions I, II and III is given by : (I : r < a, II : a < r < b, III : r > b)
solution:
In region I, due to charge at centre, electric field will be present.

But in region II, electric field will be zero as electric field inside material of conductor is zero.

Again for outside region, net charges enclosed is not zero and therefore electric field will exist.

subject: Physics
q:
The gravitational field in a region is given by: $\vec{E}=(5 N / k g) \hat{i}+(12 N / k g) \hat{j}$
If the potential at the origin is taken to be zero, then the ratio of the potential at the points $(12 \mathrm{~m}, 0)$ and $(0,5 \mathrm{~m})$ is :
question:
The gravitational field in a region is given by: $\vec{E}=(5 N / k g) \hat{i}+(12 N / k g) \hat{j}$
If the potential at the origin is taken to be zero, then the ratio of the potential at the points $(12 \mathrm{~m}, 0)$ and $(0,5 \mathrm{~m})$ is :
solution:
From question, $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{x}}=5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{kg}$ and $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{y}}=12 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{kg}$
Gravitational potential
$=$ Gravitational field $\times$ distance
$\therefore \mathrm{V}_{(12 \mathrm{~m}, 0)}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{x}} \times 12 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}$
and $\mathrm{V}_{(0,5 \mathrm{~m})}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{y}} \times 5 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}$
(Given : potential at the origin is zero)
$$
\therefore \frac{V_{(12 \mathrm{~m}, 0)}}{\mathrm{V}_{(0,5 \mathrm{~m})}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{x}} \times 12}{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{y}} \times 5}=\frac{5 \times 12}{12 \times 5}=1
$$

subject: Physics
q:
A spherically symmetric charge distribution is characterised by a charge density having the following variations:
$\rho(r)=\rho_o\left(1-\frac{r}{R}\right)$ for $r < R$
$\rho(\mathrm{r})=0$ for $r \geq \mathrm{R}$
Where $r$ is the distance from the centre of the charge distribution $\rho_{\mathrm{o}}$ is a constant. The electric field at an internal point $(r < R)$ is:
question:
A spherically symmetric charge distribution is characterised by a charge density having the following variations:
$\rho(r)=\rho_o\left(1-\frac{r}{R}\right)$ for $r < R$
$\rho(\mathrm{r})=0$ for $r \geq \mathrm{R}$
Where $r$ is the distance from the centre of the charge distribution $\rho_{\mathrm{o}}$ is a constant. The electric field at an internal point $(r < R)$ is:
solution:
Let us consider a spherical shell of radius $x$ and thickness $\mathrm{dx}$.
Charge on this shell $$ d q=\rho .4 \pi x^2 d x=\rho_0\left(1-\frac{x}{R}\right) \cdot 4 \pi x^2 d x $$ $\therefore$ Total charge in the spherical region from centre to $r(r < R)$ is $$ \begin{aligned} &q=\int d q=4 \pi \rho_0 \int_0^r\left(1-\frac{x}{R}\right) x^2 d x \\ &=4 \pi \rho_0\left[\frac{x^3}{3}-\frac{x^4}{4 R}\right]_0^r \\ &=4 \pi \rho_0\left[\frac{r^3}{3}-\frac{r^4}{4 R}\right] \\ &=4 \pi \rho_0 r^3\left[\frac{1}{3}-\frac{r}{4 R}\right] \end{aligned} $$ $\therefore$ Electric field at $\mathrm{r}, \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{r}^2}$ $$ \begin{aligned} &=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{4 \pi \rho_0 r^3}{r^2}\left[\frac{1}{3}-\frac{r}{4 R}\right] \\ &=\frac{\rho_0}{\varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{r}{3}-\frac{r^2}{4 R}\right] \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements.
Statement I : Electric potential is constant within and at the surface of each conductor.

Statement II : Electric field just outside a charged conductor is perpendicular to the surface of the conductor at every point.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options give below.
question:
Given below are two statements.
Statement I : Electric potential is constant within and at the surface of each conductor.

Statement II : Electric field just outside a charged conductor is perpendicular to the surface of the conductor at every point.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options give below.
options: Both statement I and statement II are correct
Both statement I and statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but and statement II is correct
solution:
Statement - I: True as body of conductor acts as equipotential surface.

Statement II: True, as conductor is equipotential. Tangential component of electric field should be zero. Therefore, electric field should be perpendicular to surface.

subject: Physics
q: A three coulomb charge moves from the point $(0, -2, -5)$ to the point $(5, 1, 2)$ in an electric field expressed as $\vec{E} = 2x\hat{i} + 3y^2\hat{j} + 4\hat{k}$ N/C. The work done in moving the charge is _______ J.
question: A three coulomb charge moves from the point $(0, -2, -5)$ to the point $(5, 1, 2)$ in an electric field expressed as $\vec{E} = 2x\hat{i} + 3y^2\hat{j} + 4\hat{k}$ N/C. The work done in moving the charge is _______ J.
solution:
The force experienced by the charge in the electric field is given by $\vec{F} = q\vec{E}$.

Given $q = 3$ C and $\vec{E} = 2x\hat{i} + 3y^2\hat{j} + 4\hat{k}$ N/C, the force is:
$\vec{F} = 3(2x\hat{i} + 3y^2\hat{j} + 4\hat{k}) = 6x\hat{i} + 9y^2\hat{j} + 12\hat{k}$ N

The work done by the electric field in moving the charge is:
$W = \int \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{r} = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F_x dx + \int_{y_1}^{y_2} F_y dy + \int_{z_1}^{z_2} F_z dz$

Substituting the limits from the initial point $(0, -2, -5)$ to the final point $(5, 1, 2)$:
$W = \int_{0}^{5} 6x dx + \int_{-2}^{1} 9y^2 dy + \int_{-5}^{2} 12 dz$

Evaluating each integral separately:
$\int_{0}^{5} 6x dx = \left[ 3x^2 \right]_{0}^{5} = 3(5^2 - 0^2) = 75$ J

$\int_{-2}^{1} 9y^2 dy = \left[ 3y^3 \right]_{-2}^{1} = 3(1^3 - (-2)^3) = 3(1 - (-8)) = 3(9) = 27$ J

$\int_{-5}^{2} 12 dz = \left[ 12z \right]_{-5}^{2} = 12(2 - (-5)) = 12(7) = 84$ J

Total work done $W = 75 + 27 + 84 = 186$ J.

Answer: $186$

subject: Physics
q: A simple pendulum of length L is placed between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor having electric field  E , as shown in figure. Its bob has mass m and charge  q . The time period of the pendulum is given by:
question: A simple pendulum of length L is placed between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor having electric field  E , as shown in figure. Its bob has mass m and charge  q . The time period of the pendulum is given by:
solution:
Two forces will be acting on the mass m, gravity force and force by the electric field.

Thus, the effective force =  qE2+mg2
From force equation, geff=g2+qEm2
Time period of the pendulum is T=2πLgeff
=2π Lg2+qEm2

subject: Physics
q: Sixty four conducting drops each of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and each carrying a charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> are combined to form a bigger drop. The ratio of surface density of bigger drop to the smaller drop will be
question: Sixty four conducting drops each of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and each carrying a charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> are combined to form a bigger drop. The ratio of surface density of bigger drop to the smaller drop will be
solution:
Total volume will be constant. Therefore, n4π3r3=4π3R3⇒6413r=R⇒R=4r

Final surface charge density σ'=nσ04πr24πR2=64×σ0r216r2⇒σ'σ0=41

subject: Physics
q: A square loop of sides $a=1 \mathrm{~m}$ is held normally in front of a point charge $\mathrm{q}=1 \mathrm{C}$. The flux of the electric field through the shaded region is $\frac{5}{\mathrm{p}} \times \frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \frac{\mathrm{Nm}^2}{\mathrm{C}}$, where the value of p is _____.
question: A square loop of sides $a=1 \mathrm{~m}$ is held normally in front of a point charge $\mathrm{q}=1 \mathrm{C}$. The flux of the electric field through the shaded region is $\frac{5}{\mathrm{p}} \times \frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \frac{\mathrm{Nm}^2}{\mathrm{C}}$, where the value of p is _____.
solution: Total flux through square $=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{\epsilon_0}\left(\frac{1}{6}\right)$ Lets divide square is 8 equal parts. Flux is same for each part. $\therefore$ Flux through shaded portion is $\frac{5}{8}$ (Total flux) $=\frac{5}{8} \times \frac{\mathrm{q}}{\epsilon_0} \frac{1}{6}=\frac{5}{48} \frac{1}{\epsilon_0}$ $\therefore$ required Ans. is 48

subject: Physics
q: A point charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is moved from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> in a uniform electric field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> directed along positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. If coordinates of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> respectively, the work done by electric field will be
question: A point charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is moved from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> in a uniform electric field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> directed along positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. If coordinates of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> respectively, the work done by electric field will be
solution:
As electric field is uniform, so force acting on the charged particle will be uniform.

Therefore,

WE=F→·s→=qE→·s→

=2×10-230i^·0i^+0j^+0k^-i^+2j^+0k^

=-60×10-2 J

=-600 mJ

subject: Physics
q: Four equal point charges <math><mi>Q</mi></math> each are placed in the <math><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi></math> plane at <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> . The work required to put a fifth charge <math><mi>Q</mi></math> at the origin of the coordinate system will be:
question: Four equal point charges <math><mi>Q</mi></math> each are placed in the <math><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi></math> plane at <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> . The work required to put a fifth charge <math><mi>Q</mi></math> at the origin of the coordinate system will be:
solution:
Work done in bringing the fifth charge at origin is equal to change in the potential energy of the system of charges.

Wext=ΔU=Uf-Ui if  Ui=0 (assumed)

Wext=Uf-0=KQ22+KQ22+KQ225+KQ225

Hence, the work done in bringing the fifth charge at the origin is 
Wext=Q24πε01+15.

subject: Physics
q: A small uncharged conducting sphere is placed in contact with an identical sphere but having $4 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C}$ charge and then removed to a distance such that the force of repulsion between them is $9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~N}$. The distance between them is (Take $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}$ as $9 \times 10^9 \mathrm{in} \mathrm{SI}$ units)
question: A small uncharged conducting sphere is placed in contact with an identical sphere but having $4 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C}$ charge and then removed to a distance such that the force of repulsion between them is $9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~N}$. The distance between them is (Take $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}$ as $9 \times 10^9 \mathrm{in} \mathrm{SI}$ units)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{k}\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)}{\mathrm{r}^2} \\ & 9 \times 10^{-3}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times\left(4 \times 10^{-8}\right) \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}{4 \times \mathrm{r}^2} \\ & \mathrm{r}^2=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 16 \times 10^{-16}}{4 \times 9 \times 10^{-3}}=4 \times 10^{-4} \\ & \mathrm{r}=2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A uniformly charged ring of radius <math><mn>3</mn><mi>a</mi></math> and total charge <math><mi>q</mi></math> is placed in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>‐</mo><mi>y</mi></math> plane centred at origin. A point charge <math><mi>q</mi></math> is moving towards the ring along the <math><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo></math> axis and has speed <math><mi>v</mi></math> at <math><mi> </mi><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>a</mi></math> . The minimum value of <math><mi>v</mi></math> such that it crosses the origin is:
question: A uniformly charged ring of radius <math><mn>3</mn><mi>a</mi></math> and total charge <math><mi>q</mi></math> is placed in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>‐</mo><mi>y</mi></math> plane centred at origin. A point charge <math><mi>q</mi></math> is moving towards the ring along the <math><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo></math> axis and has speed <math><mi>v</mi></math> at <math><mi> </mi><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>a</mi></math> . The minimum value of <math><mi>v</mi></math> such that it crosses the origin is:
solution:
From the conservation of energy,
14πε0q23a2+4a2+12mv2=14πε0q23a
v=q24πε0a×215×2m
=2m215q24πε0a1/2

subject: Physics
q: A stream of a positively charged particles having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="italic">q</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">m</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>kg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is deflected by an electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi>kV</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The electric field exists in a region of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> direction. Due to the electric field, the deflection of the charge particles in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> direction is _____  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mm</mi></math>.
question: A stream of a positively charged particles having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="italic">q</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">m</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>kg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is deflected by an electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi>kV</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The electric field exists in a region of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> direction. Due to the electric field, the deflection of the charge particles in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> direction is _____  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mm</mi></math>.
solution:
Acceleration of the particles due to electric field, a=Fm=qEm=2×10111.8×103

=3.6×1014 m s-2

Time to cross plates =dv

⇒t=0.103×107

Now deflection,

y=12at2=123.6×10140.103×1072

=0.002 m

=2 mm

subject: Physics
q: Two point charges $+8 q$ and $-2 q$ are located at $x=0$ and $x=L$ respectively. The location of a point on the $x$ axis at which the net electric field due to these two point charges is zero is
question: Two point charges $+8 q$ and $-2 q$ are located at $x=0$ and $x=L$ respectively. The location of a point on the $x$ axis at which the net electric field due to these two point charges is zero is
solution:
$-\frac{\mathrm{k} 2 q}{(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{L})^2}+\frac{\mathrm{k} 8 \mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{x}^2}=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=2 \mathrm{~L}$
